elements.sent-2411-001: Recent advances in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and Graph Transformers (GTs) have been driven by innovations in architectures and Positional Encodings (PEs), which are critical for augmenting node features and capturing graph topology.
elements.sent-2411-002: PEs are essential for GTs, where topological information would otherwise be lost without message-passing.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, PEs are often tested alongside novel architectures, making it difficult to isolate their effect on established models.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address this, we present a comprehensive benchmark of PEs in a unified framework that includes both message-passing GNNs and GTs.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also establish theoretical connections between MPNNs and GTs and introduce a sparsified GRIT attention mechanism to examine the influence of global connectivity.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our findings demonstrate that previously untested combinations of GNN architectures and PEs can outperform existing methods and offer a more comprehensive picture of the state-of-the-art.
elements.sent-2411-007: To support future research and experimentation in our framework, we make the code publicly available.
elements.sent-2411-008: Graph machine learning has traditionally relied on message-passing neural networks (MPNNs), which work through iterative rounds of neighborhood aggregation~\citep{kipf2016semi .}
elements.sent-2411-009: In each round, nodes update their states by incorporating information from their neighbors along with their own current states.
elements.sent-2411-010: While effective in capturing local graph structures, this approach can struggle with modeling long-range dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-011: Graph Transformer (GT) architectures utilize full attention mechanisms to circumvent this, but necessitate new methods to integrate graph topology information~\citep{dwivedi2020generalization .}
elements.sent-2411-012: This is similar to how positional encodings (PEs) in Natural Language Processing (NLP) represent token positions within sequences~\citep{vaswani2017attention .}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, encoding positional information in graphs is more complex than in sequences.
elements.sent-2411-014: Ideally, positional encodings should allow the reconstruction of the graph’s topology from node features and provide useful inductive biases to improve performance~\citep{black2024comparing .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Despite the growing number of new graph transformer architectures and positional encodings, there has been a lack of systematic evaluation comparing these encodings across different GT architectures.
elements.sent-2411-016: This makes it difficult to determine whether observed performance improvements are due to novel encodings or architectural innovations.
elements.sent-2411-017: In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of various positional encodings for both message-passing and transformer frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-018: Our goal is to understand the impact of positional encodings on model performance and identify the best combinations of encodings and architectures.
elements.sent-2411-019: By benchmarking state-of-the-art graph transformers with a variety of positional encodings, we provide a clear picture of the current state of the field and offer guidance for future research.
elements.sent-2411-020: Additionally, we further strengthen the theoretical connection between MPNNs and GTs.
elements.sent-2411-021: Although GTs are generally considered fundamentally different due to their use of attention mechanisms, we show that under certain conditions, MPNNs and GTs can be equally expressive, with additional results that extend the scope of previous analyses~\citep{velivckovic2023everything, muller2024aligning .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Specifically, MPNNs can be applied to fully-connected graphs and operate like a GT, while attention mechanisms can also be adapted for local message-passing.
elements.sent-2411-023: Our theoretical analysis demonstrates that both MPNNs and GTs can have the same expressiveness when the underlying topology of the MPNN is fully connected.
elements.sent-2411-024: Based on these insights, we extend our evaluation to include MPNNs with positional encodings on fully-connected graphs and modify state-of-the-art attention mechanisms for localized graph convolutions.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our results indicate that by combining existing positional encodings and architectures, state-of-the-art performance can be achieved on several benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-026: Our contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-027: We conduct an empirical evaluation of various positional encodings across message-passing neural networks and Graph Transformers to consolidate the current state-of-the-art and find new combinations that surpass the previous best models.
elements.sent-2411-028: We provide theoretical insights into the relationship between MPNNs and GTs, showing conditions where they share similar expressiveness.
elements.sent-2411-029: Based on these observations, we introduce a sparsified version of GRIT attention for localized graph convolutions, which proves effective across multiple datasets.
elements.sent-2411-030: We provide a unified evaluation framework implementing all used architectures and positional encodings in one codebase to facilitate the testing of new positional encodings and models.
elements.sent-2411-032: Earlier graph neural networks (GNNs), including models like GCN \citep{kipf2016semi , GAT \citep{velivckovic2017graph , GraphSAGE \citep{hamilton2017inductive , and GIN \citep{xu2018powerful , have paved the way for various advancements.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Some convolutional filtering variants incorporate edge attributes into their architecture.
elements.sent-2411-034: GatedGCN \citep{bresson2017residual employs gates as sparse attention mechanisms, while GINE \citep{hu2019strategies augments features with local edges.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Recent efforts aim to enhance the expressive power of GNNs, addressing the limitations imposed by the 1-WL test.
elements.sent-2411-036: For instance, Principal Neighborhood Aggregation (PNA) \citep{corso2020principal combines different aggregators with degree-scalers to tackle isomorphism tasks in continuous feature spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Higher-order GNNs, like k-GNN \citep{morris2019weisfeiler, maron2019provably , build on the k-WL algorithm, a more generalized version of the WL test, offering increased expressive power.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Other approaches, such as GSN \citep{bouritsas2022improving and GIN-AK+ \citep{zhao2021stars , utilize substructures (subgraphs) for message passing, while methods like CIN \citep{bodnar2021weisfeiler operate on regular cell complexes, although they remain less powerful than the 3-WL test.}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Importantly, these models serve as baselines in some graph transformers, demonstrating comparable performance with certain GTs, as cited in GraphGPS \citep{rampavsek2022recipe , GRIT \citep{ma2023graph , and Exphormer \citep{shirzad2023exphormer .}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Graph Transformers (GT) were popularized in recent years \citep{rampavsek2022recipe, liu2023towards, mao2024graph, zhang2023graph .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Modules including positional or structural encodings, global attention, and local message passing are considered as mainstream design components for a standard graph transformer model, which successfully solved the problem of in-scalability \citep{rampavsek2022recipe, shirzad2023exphormer in large graphs, lack of graph inductive bias \citep{ma2023graph , and over-smoothing problems \citep{chen2022nagphormer .}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Apart from its maturity in some machine learning fields such as natural language processing, computer vision, or bioinformatics that many previous GT papers have mentioned, GTs have also demonstrated their strength by extending their application to scientific domains such as differential equations \citep{bryutkin2024hamlet, choromanski2022block , quantum physics \citep{wang2022graph , and symbolic regression \citep{zhonggraph .}}}

elements.sent-2411-045: MPNNs and GTs have been compared extensively in the literature, with early work observing that these models can simulate one another~\citep{velivckovic2023everything .}
elements.sent-2411-046: A more rigorous theoretical analysis has demonstrated that GTs can be related to MPNNs when a virtual node is employed~\citep{cai2023connection .}

elements.sent-2411-050: We provide an in-depth review of positional encodings and benchmarking in Section~\ref{sec:positinoal_encodings and Appendix~\ref{sec:extended_related_work .}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Numerous positional encodings for graph-based models have been discussed in recent research, but they are often scattered across various ablation studies with no unified framework.
elements.sent-2411-052: In this paper, we categorize and streamline the formal definition of existing graph-based positional encodings into three main categories: Laplacian-based, Random walk-based, and others.
elements.sent-2411-053: We start with some fundamental definitions related to graphs.

elements.subsection-2411-010: Running Time and Memory Complexity for PEs
elements.sent-2411-192: The computational cost of positional encodings (PEs) is a critical consideration, particularly for large graphs where methods with high complexity quickly become infeasible.
elements.sent-2411-193: We evaluated the running time and memory usage for various PEs, and the full results are presented in Appendix~\ref{sec:running_times .}
elements.sent-2411-194: RRWP is the most memory-intensive PE, but maintains reasonable running times.
elements.sent-2411-195: RWSE and RWDIFF, on the other hand, tend to have significantly longer running times but are relatively more memory-efficient.
elements.sent-2411-196: Laplacian-based methods, such as LapPE and ESLapPE, offer a good balance between computational speed and memory usage, making them practical even for larger datasets.
elements.sent-2411-197: PPR and GCKN come with high computational demands in both time and memory, making them less suited for large-scale graphs.
elements.sent-2411-198: In contrast, Laplacian-based encodings like ESLapPE strike a better trade-off, making them practical for a broader range of graph sizes while still offering competitive performance.
elements.sent-2411-199: For superpixel graph datasets, such as PascalVOC-SP, COCO-SP, MNIST, and CIFAR10, we found that the inclusion of positional encodings generally does not result in substantial performance improvements.
elements.sent-2411-200: In particular, larger superpixel graphs like PascalVOC-SP and COCO-SP showed minimal to no gains from adding PEs, while MNIST similarly exhibited negligible benefits.
elements.sent-2411-201: An exception to this trend is CIFAR10, where RRWP demonstrated potential for enhancing model performance.
elements.sent-2411-202: This suggests that while superpixel graphs may not typically benefit from positional encodings, RRWP could be considered as a candidate for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-203: However, the gains observed may not always justify the increased computational complexity associated with RRWP for such datasets.
elements.sent-2411-204: In contrast, molecular datasets, such as ZINC and the Peptides variations, displayed a strong dependency on the choice of positional encoding, with significant variations in model performance based on the PE used.
elements.sent-2411-205: For instance, ZINC consistently showed the best results with PPR.
elements.sent-2411-206: On the other hand, the Peptides datasets revealed task-specific preferences: Peptides-func benefited the most from RRWP, while Peptides-struct achieved optimal performance with WLPE.
elements.sent-2411-207: Interestingly, despite using identical graph structures, the two Peptides tasks favored different PEs, which indicates that the nature of the prediction target (functional vs. structural) plays a significant role.
elements.sent-2411-208: Thus, when dealing with molecular datasets, practitioners are advised to experiment with various PEs, as the optimal choice may depend more on the specific task than on the graph structure itself.
elements.sent-2411-209: Still, the optimal PE for a given dataset is generally consistent across different models, which, in combination with the fact that we test on commonly used datasets provides practitioners with a strong starting point for their experiments.
elements.sent-2411-210: This distinction is further highlighted when comparing random-walk-based encodings with Laplacian encodings, where one typically emerges as the clear winner depending on the dataset and task.
elements.sent-2411-211: This study underscores the critical role of positional encodings in enhancing the performance of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), particularly within Graph Transformer architectures.
elements.sent-2411-212: We conducted a thorough comparison of various positional encodings across a wide array of state-of-the-art models and identify the optimal configurations for diverse datasets, as well as offer valuable insights into the relationship between positional encodings and model performance.
elements.sent-2411-213: While we consolidated much of the current state-of-the-art, we also identified new configurations that surpass the performance of existing best models, such as Exphormer on PCQM-Contact.
elements.sent-2411-214: This underscores the necessity of in-depth comparisons to provide a fair and accurate ranking.
elements.sent-2411-215: Our theoretical considerations have led to the development of the SparseGRIT model.
elements.sent-2411-216: This model shows competitive performance across multiple benchmarks, while maintaining scalability to larger graphs.
elements.sent-2411-217: It shows that sparse message-passing together with the right positional encodings is a viable option on many datasets.
elements.sent-2411-218: Furthermore, we provide a comprehensive overview of the current state-of-the-art in graph learning and highlight the importance of selecting appropriate positional encodings to achieve optimal results.
elements.sent-2411-219: Our unified codebase includes implementations of all tested models and encodings, which serves as a valuable resource for future research.
elements.sent-2411-220: The framework ensures reproducible results and supports the integration of new datasets, models, and positional encodings, thereby facilitating further experimentation.
elements.sent-2411-221: Due to computational constraints, we could not explore all possible hyperparameter configurations and there might be slightly better performing ones that we did not catch.
elements.sent-2411-222: Additionally, although we tested a wide range of models and encodings, it is infeasible to test every model and PE.
elements.sent-2411-223: This is why we focused on current state-of-the-art for both.
elements.sent-2411-224: Further, our evaluations are based on a specific set of benchmark datasets, which may not fully represent the diversity of real-world graph structures.
elements.sent-2411-225: Thus, performance on these benchmarks may not generalize to all types of graph data.
elements.sent-2411-226: Nevertheless, our unified codebase serves as a robust foundation for further testing and development, and enables researchers to overcome these limitations by facilitating the inclusion of new datasets, models, and positional encodings.. \bibliographystyle{iclr2025_conference .}
elements.sent-2411-227: Positional encodings are traditionally used in natural language processing to capture the absolute position of a token within a sentence \citep{vaswani2017attention or the relative distance between pairs of tokens \citep{shaw2018self, ke2020rethinking, chen2021permuteformer .}}
elements.sent-2411-228: Similarly, positional encoding in graphs aims to learn both local topology and global structural information of nodes efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-229: This approach has been successfully implemented with the introduction of the graph transformer \citep{dwivedi2020generalization .}
elements.sent-2411-230: With the advent of graph transformers in the field of graph representation learning, many traditional graph theory methods have been revitalized.
elements.sent-2411-231: Graph signal processing techniques have been employed such as Laplacian decomposition and finite hop random walks \citep{rampavsek2022recipe, dwivedi2023benchmarking, ma2023graph, beaini2021directional, dwivedi2020generalization, kreuzer2021rethinking, dwivedi2021graph, lim2022sign, wang2022equivariant as absolute or relative positional encoding.}
elements.sent-2411-232: Node properties such as degree centrality \citep{ying2021transformers and personalized PageRank (PPR) \citep{gasteiger2018predict,fu2024vcr could be mapped and expanded into higher dimensions for absolute positional encoding, while the shortest distance between nodes could be used for relative positional encoding \citep{li2020distance, ying2021transformers .}}}
elements.sent-2411-233: Recent studies have focused on developing learnable positional encodings for graphs \citep{ying2021transformers and exploring their expressiveness and stability as well \citep{wang2022equivariant, ma2023graph, huang2023stability .}}
elements.sent-2411-234: Additionally, graph rewiring combined with layout optimization to coarsen graphs has been proposed as a form of positional encoding \citep{grotschla2024core .}
elements.sent-2411-235: One of the first GNN benchmarking papers compared architectures with and without positional encodings (PEs) \citep{dwivedi2023benchmarking , where their PE mainly refers to Laplacian positional encoding (LapPE).}
elements.sent-2411-236: Their study was limited to the GatedGCN model and discussed the expressive power, robustness, and efficiency of state-of-the-art message-passing methods.
elements.sent-2411-237: Additionally, several surveys have benchmarked the complexity, specific tasks, unified message-passing frameworks \citep{wu2020comprehensive, zhou2020graph , robustness, and privacy \citep{ju2024survey .}}
elements.sent-2411-238: The LRGB dataset~\citep{dwivedi2022long has been tested in both GNNs and transformers to demonstrate the superiority of Graph Transformers (GTs) over Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs).}
elements.sent-2411-239: Many state-of-the-art GTs have included this benchmark in their experiments \citep{rampavsek2022recipe, shirzad2023exphormer,ma2023graph .}

elements.sent-2411-243: For message passing neural networks, we primarily choose GatedGCN \citep{bresson2017residual and GINE \citep{hu2019strategies .}}

elements.sent-2411-284: We consider three Laplacian-based methods: Laplacian Positional Encoding (LapPE), Sign-Invariant Positional Encoding (SignNet), and rectified Graph Convolution Kernel Network-based Positional Encoding (GCKN).
elements.sent-2411-285: LapPE, or Laplacian Positional Encoding, is a method that leverages the eigenvectors of the graph Laplacian to encode positional information for nodes.
elements.sent-2411-286: The core idea is that the eigenvectors corresponding to higher eigenvalues contain more information about the local structure of the graph, especially the relationships between a node and its neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-287: We can further concatenate the eigenvectors with their corresponding eigenvalue.
elements.sent-2411-288: In the actual implementation, an additional parameter $\mathcal{S}$ is employed to randomly split the sign of this concatenated eigenvector.
elements.sent-2411-289: Subsequently, we apply either a DeepSet \citep{zaheer2017deep or an MLP $\Phi$ parameterized by $\Theta$ to this eigenvector. \begin{equation}X^{k}_{PE} = f\left( U_{k, :}, \Lambda, \Theta, S\right) = \Phi_{\Theta} \left(S \odot \left( U_{k, :} \mathbin{\|} \Lambda_k \right)\right)\end{equation} The equivariant and stable version of LapPE (ESLapPE) \citep{wang2022equivariant follows the same procedure but omits the post-processing MLP.}}
elements.sent-2411-290: SignNet is an advanced version of LapPE, which considers both the original eigenvector and its inversely signed counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-291: An additional graph neural network (GNN) is applied to capture local Laplacian signals before passing them to the MLP.
elements.sent-2411-292: The outputs from two distinct or shared GNNs are then added together.
